docs.title: Major Components
docs.text: The Trusk Empire was primarily composed of three major kingdoms: index, also known as the Northern Land Kingdom, mainly consisting of the territories of the nomadic Glömezher tribes in the north; index, the core territory of the empire, also known as the Heimet Kingdom, later divided into the Heimet and Tyesuku kingdoms; Laprio, also known as the Novaya Kingdom. "Novaya" means "seaside" in ancient Laprio language.

docs.title: Origins: The Beginning of the Artisan Nation
Trusk City-States: The Establishment of Civilization
docs.text:
According to mythology, the Trusk tribes should also have migrated down from the Lakme Saumen region to the Trusk Basin and surrounding areas.

Including the Trusk city-state, there were many city-states of various sizes within the Trusk region, scattered among the complex hills of the Trusk Basin. Most of these city-states originated from cities near rivers, gradually expanding their respective boundaries until their territories began to overlap. The territory of Trusk that we speculate today included rich mineral mountains and rivers from which fragmented ore could be panned. This time is too far from the present, and there are no very clear historical records. However, considering that detailed records of metal forging already existed during the subsequent Kuileot Dynasty, Trusk at that time—as their given name suggests—should have been quite skilled at forging metal. This is also considered the main reason for Trusk's rise and eventual dominance.

Besides Trusk, there were many other city-states within the basin, the most important of which was probably Bagunalo. Their territory was mainly concentrated around the largest freshwater lake in the Trusk region, quite close to the core of the Trusk city-state. The prototype of the God Taciap among the six main gods likely originated from this city-state. They also had a long history of friction with the Trusk city-state, ultimately succumbing to Trusk's superior weapons and soldiers. After conquering Bagunalo, the largest grain-producing area in Trusk, military supplies were no longer a problem for Trusk. Several nearby city-states surrendered one after another, while more distant tribes and city-states maintained a wait-and-see attitude. Given Trusk's transportation level at the time, the range Trusk could effectively control was probably relatively limited. After being conquered, Bagunalo ceased to exist as a political unit but was remembered and preserved as a place name.

After gaining control of several surrounding city-states, the Trusk people seemed to have lost the use for their forging technology for a time. However, they soon turned to technological innovation. Based on archaeological discoveries, it is speculated that they may have begun using primitive mechanical components such as pulleys and iron chains. Building on this, the Trusk people launched a large-scale land reclamation movement and systematically constructed infrastructure such as canals and roads within the territory of what is now the New Trusk city-state. These construction achievements quickly gave them the capability for long-distance expeditions, ultimately expanding their rule to the entire Trusk Basin.

docs.title: The First King: KuileotKuileot
docs.text:
In contemporary Truskean, Kuileot not only exists as a surname but has also evolved into KuilemKuilem, a term for high-ranking nobles. There are various legends about the origin of King Kuileot's name. The first hypothesis suggests that an early city-state conquered by the Trusk city-state worshipped a deity named Kuileot. During the long process of integration, Kuileot gradually evolved into a general term for the nobles of that city-state. However, the current Trusk-Heimet pantheon does not include the name Kuileot. Therefore, another theory states that the word originally meant "gold" in ancient Truskean—Kuileot might have been a general term for merchants traveling between city-states at the time. These merchants were accustomed to using gold for transactions and carried gold objects, hence the name; some of these merchants later became strategists, playing an important role in Trusk's wars to unify the northwest of the empire. The "gold" hypothesis has another explanation: based on inscription research, this might have been an ancient honorific for the king, symbolizing that he possessed the most gold in the world. In summary, Kuileot was a general term for a highly influential political force during the ancient Trusk city-state era, and this force eventually coalesced into King Kuileot.

Kuileot is the first recorded monarch of Trusk. Before him, Trusk seems not to have formed a tradition of recording history. Although the content of unearthed inscriptions is quite detailed, major events might have occurred earlier, leading to the destruction of all early royal inscriptions. Inscriptions record that during his reign, Kuileot gathered treasures from the world, issued decrees, built palaces, and mentioned wars with Heimet, but did not detail the specific circumstances of the battles. Judging from the inscription's record that "treasures were nearly exhausted," this war should have been quite brutal. Ultimately, Kuileot successfully conquered Heimet, becoming the overlord of all city-states along the two great rivers from Trusk to the strait.

Several monarchs who succeeded Kuileot also left inscriptions, recording the economic and political conditions of the time, although the content is relatively brief. It is worth noting that the names of these early successors are not related to Kuileot; one was even named "Liekukya" (meaning "the one who roasts meat"). However, starting from the fourth monarch, all royal titles were prefixed with "Kuileot," so this period ultimately became known as the Kuileot Dynasty—although the term "Kuileot" itself might also have been merely a general term for "monarch."

The Kuileot Dynasty lasted for a considerable period, now generally believed to be around eight hundred years. During this time, agricultural production methods were still at a relatively primitive stage, capable of generating a certain amount of accumulation but unable to provide a stable foundation for the dynasty's rule; barbarian tribes occasionally harassed several remote small city-states, plundering grain, population, and equipment; the accumulation of materials formed some basic exchange but had not yet developed into a trade system. In simple terms, the productivity level of this period did not provide the material foundation required for long-term and stable rule by those in power; although the core political system remained stable, no definitive evidence of smooth succession to the throne has been found, making it impossible to judge whether the regime changes during this period were truly as recorded in the inscriptions; it is also unknown whether there were actual blood relationships between kings or some kind of abdication and inheritance system. This perhaps also sowed the seeds for the dynasty's end.

docs.title: The Wrath of the Sea God: HeimetHeimet Dynasty
docs.text:
The name Heimet also originated from one of the ancient city-states in the Trusk region, but the Heimet of the late Kuileot Dynasty was not entirely the same concept as the Heimet of the original city-state era. The original Heimet city was north of Serk Mountain, along the old Pernan River, but that Heimet had been destroyed by floods. It was replaced by the present-day Heimet south of Serk Mountain, still along the Pernan River but near Heimet Bay. By the end of the Kuileot Dynasty, Heimet had already become the largest port in the north. Trusk's colonization of the western coast of the continent was particularly prosperous during this period. Various materials were collected and distributed at Heimet Port, and Heimet's commerce reached unprecedented prosperity, leading to its continuous growth in scale and political status. This was also the economic foundation that ultimately allowed Heimet to seize the opportunity to raise an army.

The fall of the Kuileot Dynasty was similar to the dynastic changes of many in history, influenced by the cyclical fluctuations of the political situation and agricultural production. According to historical records, the late dynasty may have suffered from sustained large-scale droughts, severely damaging the agricultural economy. At the same time, doubts about the legitimacy of the Kuileot king's bloodline also emerged within the court, but due to the fragmentary nature of related records, specific details are difficult to verify. Under the intertwining of multiple crises, the Kuileot Dynasty quickly fell into comprehensive turmoil and ultimately declined.

According to several inscriptions from the Heimet period, the turmoil first began in the south. Several border city-states in northern Laprio, which had long-standing conflicts with the Trusk city-state, took advantage of the chaos to invade the southern part of the Trusk Basin. While the Kuileot army was concentrated on dealing with the Laprio people and had no extra troops, the Heimet city lord MataasioksaamenMataasioksaamen raised an army in the name of the sea god to campaign against Kuileot. The Heimet army did not expend much effort to capture half of Trusk's core cities, captured the then-young Kuileot king, and successfully cut off the grain supply from various cities to the Kuileot army. Soon, the Kuileot army disintegrated due to lack of provisions. After consolidating the various forces within Trusk, the Heimet army quickly moved south to repel the Laprio army and probably captured one or two cities along the way.

It is speculated that Trusk subsequently fell into a complex power struggle: on the surface, the young monarch of the Kuileot family remained the nominal ruler, but actual power was held by Mataasioksaamen. With the defeat, most of the Kuileot family's power had already collapsed. However, within Trusk's core regions, especially the Kuileot city-state, a large number of remnants still existed. The nobles who had previously fought among themselves also united at this time to jointly resist Heimet's rule. Meanwhile, the old nobles who had been suppressed by the Kuileot family also regrouped, forming a force that could not be ignored. In the following years, rebellions frequently broke out within the empire. To maintain rule, the Heimet army had to be stationed long-term in the Trusk Basin for suppression.

During this transitional period of frequent power struggles, while Mataasioksaamen consolidated his rule and waited for an opportunity to end the Kuileot legal tradition, he also carried out various reforms of the state. Among them, the Heimet-Trusk belief system was established during this period. Mataasioksaamen systematically organized the mythological systems of the various city-states, recompiled the pantheon, and significantly elevated the status of Heimet deities within it. Additionally, he began to improve the tax system to strengthen tax management of merchants traveling between city-states. In terms of law, Mataasioksaamen attempted to implement new legal statutes based on Heimet's original laws but ultimately failed to form a written legal code. In terms of infrastructure construction, he oversaw the construction of the Mataasioksaamen Road connecting the Heimet Strait and the Trusk Basin, and built ports in Heimet and the southern coastal areas of the Trusk Basin. These projects not only laid the foundation for the future expansion of the Trusk Empire to the western coast of the strait but also greatly facilitated the transmission of information and flow of materials between Heimet and various parts of the empire. The above series of measures are collectively referred to by later generations as the "Mataasioksaamen Reforms."

Mataasioksaamen ultimately did not ascend to the throne of Trusk. Mataasioksaamen's son, Mataasioksaamen II, finally ascended the throne as the son-in-law of the Kuileot family, and the Heimet Dynasty began.

Building on the foundation of the Kuileot Dynasty, the Heimet Dynasty developed agricultural production methods, largely resolving the contradiction between the growing demand for grain and insufficient grain reserves. In commerce, the Mataasioksaamen Reforms and the construction of the Mataasioksaamen Road provided convenience for the prosperity of commercial activities. A primitive monetary system began to form, and a good balance system between the king and merchants was established. Militarily, Heimet also successfully resisted invasions by the Andon people from the northeast and pirates from the Northern Wastes. The empire's western border continuously expanded until the Anioksait jungle blocked the army's advance. The implementation of legal statutes protected the rights of ordinary people but also to some extent consolidated the interests of the ruling class, laying the groundwork for some subsequent political events in the empire.

Apart from the above events, not many major nationwide events occurred during the Heimet First Dynasty period. What is worth mentioning might be several major wars against the Andon people and the Laprio people. The Andon people were mountain dwellers in the northwest of the empire, mostly living by fishing and hunting, with simple yet fierce customs. Warriors carried shields and axes, posing a significant threat to the Heimet Dynasty.

docs.title: Southern Barbarian Invasion: Novaya Dynasty and Basib Dynasty
docs.text:
The Laprio people in the south did not develop into a systematic state during the early city-state era. The southern land was once too hot and humid for farming, and the Laprio population never achieved effective growth. However, after Heimet counterattacked and nearly flattened several northern states speaking Laprio dialects, the Laprio people began to realize the importance of unity.

During the several hundred years of the Heimet Dynasty, under the leadership of the wealthiest city-state on the western side of the Laprio Strait, the Laprio people gradually formed a loose confederation of city-states to resist the increasing oppression from the Heimet-Trusk people. There was also a period of relative peace between the two city-state confederations: Trusk people and Laprio people coexisted peacefully at the border, merchants traveled freely between Laprio and Trusk; ships sailed unimpeded in Novaya, and carriages with permits entered and exited the border without any hindrance. After purchasing high-quality iron ore and ironware produced in Trusk, the Laprio people's farming technology developed rapidly, and the amount of cultivated land increased rapidly. According to unearthed inscriptions, there might have been a not-short ice age during the mid-Heimet Dynasty period. Agricultural production in the northern Heimet regime likely stagnated for a time. During this period, Laprio, relying on the hot and humid southern climate, still ensured agricultural production and likely exported large quantities of grain to Trusk. The trade surplus during this period was likely key to the Laprio people ultimately achieving a comprehensive national strength overtake.

At this time, Trusk began to show signs of over-reliance on grain imports from the Laprio people. The Trusk king was worried about this for a long time. Heimet spent decades dealing with the Laprio people, but at this time, a high phase difference of "snapshot" suddenly appeared. The use of "magic" suddenly became rampant, causing considerable damage and public panic.

The Novaya War began in the late Heimet Dynasty. The entire conflict lasted about twenty years in total. The first seventeen years mostly involved small-scale conflicts among border nobles, while higher-intensity battles and engagements were concentrated in the final three years. The entire war was almost unremarkable. Both armies were mired in endless logistical quagmires. The Novaya army lacked a good transportation and logistics system, while the Heimet army fundamentally lacked grain and fodder supplies.

The Novaya Dynasty ultimately captured Heimet. Because they controlled the grain and fodder supply to Heimet-Trusk during this period, it was also difficult for various places in Heimet-Trusk to organize any effective resistance.

We know very little about the various details of Novaya rule. Perhaps the Novaya Dynasty did not have the habit of recording stories with inscriptions, or perhaps the later Kuileot Second Dynasty destroyed these inscriptions for political purposes. Therefore, there are not many means now to glimpse the ruling structure of the Novaya Dynasty at that time. From some books of the Kuileot Dynasty, we can know that compared to the relatively strict ruling style of the northern regime, the rulers of the Novaya Dynasty mainly pursued a state of non-interference. There were no major reforms within the empire, and the government's main duties shifted to regulating grain and corvée labor. During this period, the aristocratic ethos brought by the Heimet Dynasty also weakened. The Novaya people did not have a formed and closed aristocratic class; the upper echelon was composed of warlords and merchants. During this period, Novaya also did not have a so-called "kingrekui," although according to records from the Kuileot Second Dynasty period, there should have been such a term for the ruler (they directly borrowed the Truskean word "rekui"). However, it is likely that the "

docs.title: Nations and Dynasties
docs.text:
Abstract

This navigation primarily records the nations of Laaerad and their respective dynasties.

docs.title: Nationkwirad
docs.text: Nation Name Government Form Brief Description Trusk Empire (Trusk) Kingdom, Empire The most powerful nation in northeastern Laaerad Tyesuku (Tyesuku) Kingdom, Tribal Confederation A nomadic tribal confederation northeast of Trusk Laprio (Laporio) Kingdom, City-State League A nation south of Trusk, on the other side of the Novaya Sea

docs.title: Dynasties
docs.text: Records the various dynasties of Laaerad.

docs.title: Dynasties of the Trusk Empire
docs.text:
Listed in chronological order:

  • [[Quileo Dynasty]]
  • Heimet First Dynasty
  • Laprio Dynasty
  • Basib Dynasty
  • Moswarike Dynasty
  • Jaudzya Dynasty
  • Heimet Second Dynasty
  • Tyesuku First Dynasty
  • [[Boteszhuahu Dynasty]]
  • Heimet Third Dynasty
  • Tyesuku Dynasty

docs.title: Concept and Etymology
docs.text: The term GlömerGlömer originally means "by the river" in the Glömezher language. * Origin of the External Name: Initially, the Truskeans used this word to refer to the earliest clan they encountered, named "Glömer". This name became fixed in the Truskean language as a term for barbarians and eventually became the collective name for the northern peoples of Trusk. * Evolution of Self-Identity: Over centuries of interaction between the Glömer people and the Truskeans, especially after the original "Glömer" lineage gradually assimilated and disappeared, the Glömer people themselves gradually accepted this designation.

docs.title: Linguistic Differences
docs.text: The word GlömerGlömer in Glömezher contains the front vowel ö, which contrasts with o, a sound not present in Truskean. Therefore, in Truskean, it is often written as gulomer. The exact cause of this phonetic shift is difficult to confirm; it may have arisen from perceptual differences during the initial contact between the two groups.

docs.title: Geography
docs.text: As an important component of the northeastern corner of the Trusk Empire, the territory of the Glömer people is concentrated in the narrow corridor between the North Sea and the East Sea.

docs.title: Topography and Climate
docs.text:
The topography of the Glömer lands is complex and diverse, dominated by rolling mountains that form a unique geographical skeleton.

  • Northern Mountains: Towering mountains reach into the clouds, with perennial snow. They are not only important water conservation areas but also a natural barrier, blocking cold currents from the north, making the climate south of the mountains relatively mild (primarily temperate).
  • River Valleys: Due to the abundance of mountains, flat land is scarce. Rivers meander through the mountain ranges, nourishing the land on both banks. The vast river valleys are the foundation of animal husbandry and the main areas for population settlement and agriculture, considered the lifeline of the national economy.
  • Western Desert: Presents a completely different scene. The vast desert region has a dry climate with scarce precipitation. The surface is mostly sand dunes, gravel plains, and saline-alkali land, but it may contain unknown mineral resources.

docs.title: Ecology and Biology
docs.text:
• Vegetation: Dominated by vast grasslands (from valley grasslands to alpine meadows), covering most non-mountainous and non-desert areas. In higher altitude or more northerly regions, cold-tolerant tundra vegetation (mosses, lichens, dwarf shrubs) is distributed.
  • Animals:
    • Livestock: Primarily cattle, sheep, and horses. Horses are used for transportation and military purposes, while cattle and sheep are the foundation of food and economy.
    • Wildlife: Wolf packs are the main predators; eagles dominate the skies; foxes and wild dogs are widely distributed in various environments.

docs.title: Origins and Legends
docs.text: The most fundamental origins of the GlömerGlömer people are lost to history due to their nomadic nature and lack of historical records. According to Glömer Legends, the Glömer people were created by the goddess Ramro from her own flesh and blood, and were sheltered by the sacred mountain Karoda during the cataclysm.

docs.title: Unification: SajapyomakSajapyomak GuuchobaguGuuchobagu
docs.text:
The first "unified" dynasty of the Glömer people was called the MoswarikeMoswarike Dynasty (Horse Clan Dynasty).

  • Rise: The progenitor GuuchobaguGuuchobagu was originally a survivor who failed in steppe struggles and was enslaved. He used superstition or spiritual power to unite slaves from various tribes during a harsh winter.
  • Seizure of Power: At a major tribal gathering, he killed the drunken elders, shouted "In the name of Latroda!", and promised followers cattle and sheep. He integrated the slave class, subdued the main tribes, and proclaimed himself SajepyomakSajepyomak (Great Elder of All Under Heaven).
  • Rule: This unification was more nominal at the time, with actual power still constrained by the Great Council of EldersLapyomokzhorchi. However, he successfully established a national consciousness for the Glömer people, integrated part of the military, and promoted the slave class.

docs.title: Succession Crisis and Southern Flight
docs.text: GuuchobaguGuuchobagu died without an heir, and his brother OnguchotroOnguchotro succeeded him. After Onguchotro's death, the Great Council of Elders refused to support his son OnguchotronorOnguchotronor, instead electing a collateral line. Onguchotronor resisted but failed and was forced to flee south to [[Trusk]].

docs.title: Restoration and Rise of the MoswarikeMoswarike Dynasty
docs.text:
During his exile in Trusk, OnguchotronorOnguchotronor, with the military forces and advanced equipment of a Trusk border lord, counterattacked the Glömer lands and defeated the Great Council of Elders.

  • Reforms: Inspired by the Trusk political system, he established the sacredness and legal legitimacy of the Moswarike family's rule, weakened the power of the Great Council of Elders, transforming it into a consultative body.
  • Magical Revival: He established the JohuzayJohuzay (Priest) system. As this coincided with a period when the "snapshot" was relatively far from the origin, magical effects were significant, and the Glömer people established a mature "Spirit Language" system.
  • Surpassing Trusk: At that time, Trusk was embroiled in the wars between the Heimet and Novaya dynasties, leading to the destruction of texts. The Glömer people's proficiency in Spirit Language surpassed that of the south, laying the groundwork for the future Glömer dynasty that would establish the Trusk Empire.

docs.title: Basic Structure
docs.text:
The political units of the Glömer people are primarily divided by geography and bloodline. Due to their nomadic nature, the structure is loose, making it difficult to form stable political structures.

  • ClanKrota: The basic political unit (e.g., mountain clan, river clan).
  • FamilyShnomca: The basic social unit, practicing patriarchy, collectively owning livestock and tents.
  • Council of EldersPyomakzherchi: The leadership body of each clan. The Great Council of EldersLapyomokzhorchi exists among clans.

docs.title: Evolution of Titles
docs.text:
• ElderPyomak: Originally referred to clan leaders, later came to denote nobility.
  • SajepyomakSajepyomak (World Elder): The title for the supreme ruler of the Glömer people. After joining the Trusk Empire, it was used to refer to the emperor.
  • JupyomakJupyomak (Sun Elder): During the imperial period, the title for the Glömer king.

docs.title: Religion and Spirit Speakers
docs.text: The Glömer people do not have a strict religious system; their beliefs are sustained through Spirit SpeakersJohuzay. * Those proficient in the Spirit LanguageJohuz are called Spirit Speakers, forming a loose noble class. * They possess abilities in prophecy, healing, and communicating with spirits. Knowledge is passed down orally, with no written records.

docs.title: Economy
docs.text:
• Primarily Nomadic: Relies on herding livestock such as yaks and goats for livelihood, producing fur, tents, clothing, and daily necessities.
  • Limited Agriculture: Some cultivated land exists near rivers, mainly growing cold-resistant edible grain crops.

docs.title: Culture
docs.text:
• Tribal Culture: Combines ancestor worship and primitive religious beliefs.
  • Language: Historically, the Glömer people had no strict official language, divided into coastal and inland dialects. The Glömezher language currently documented is based on the southern coastal dialect.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: Category Entry Glömer [[Glömer Legends]] Geography [[Trusk]], Heimet, [[Mistnatira]]

docs.title: Trusk Empire
docs.text:
Wiki

Capital [[Hasit City]] Official Language [[Truskean]] Major Ethnic Groups Truskeans, Glömezher, Laprio Political System Absolute Monarchy Core Territory Both sides of the Heimet Strait

Trusk, as a regime name frequently mentioned in historical documents, specifically refers to the powerful state established during the late ancient period at the convergence of the three old continents of Laaerad. Its unique geographical location made it a natural hub connecting the eastern and western parts of the continent. It was not only a crucial trade route, facilitating the exchange of goods, technology, and ideas, but also a melting pot for multicultural fusion. Therefore, on the historical stage of the Laaerad world, Trusk always played a pivotal role due to its irreplaceable strategic position and profound influence.

docs.title: Geography
docs.text:
What is Trusk?

The territory of the Trusk Empire lies in the very center of Laaerad's eastern continent, making it a natural hub connecting various regions of the continent. It was not only a necessary passage for trade but also a center for cultural fusion. Its territory extended north to Mistnatila and south to the rugged Guduk-Athesa Mountains, which provided a solid southern defense line for the empire. The empire possessed the continent's most fertile farmland along the Novaya coast. This fertile land was the cornerstone of its prosperity, continuously providing the empire with food and wealth, supporting a large population and a powerful economy. More crucially, the Trusk Empire also firmly controlled the strategic passages between the Novaya Sea and the North Sea and South Sea—the Heimet and [[Novaya]] Straits. These key waterways gave the empire unparalleled control over maritime trade, allowing it to effectively regulate the regional economic lifeline and project its naval power. Therefore, with its exceptionally favorable geographical location and dual control over land and sea transportation arteries, the Trusk Empire became the undisputed core power on this continent.

docs.title: Topography
docs.text:
The core region of the Trusk Empire is dominated by the vast Trusk Basin, a fertile lowland that forms the heartland of the nation. A belt extending along the Novaya Sea coast consists mostly of fertile alluvial plains. Benefiting from a Mediterranean-like climate and nourishment from rivers, the soil here is particularly suitable for cultivating wheat, barley, and various vegetables, forming the empire's most important agricultural production area.

The western part of the empire is a region of towering, continuous mountain ranges, primarily divided into two systems: the northern Tyesuku Mountains are known for their steep peaks and rich mineral resources, especially silver and iron; while the southern Genara Mountains are famous for their dense coniferous forests and unique mountain ecosystems, serving as habitats for many rare animals. The northern border features hills, beyond which lie the grasslands of the Glömezher people. This vast grassland area is home to nomadic Glömezher tribes who live by herding reindeer and hunting. The northernmost Glömezher territory of the empire extends even further north to the Mistnatila region, considered sacred by the Glömezher. This perpetually snow-covered, cold area is not only their spiritual sanctuary but is also said to hold many unknown treasures from ancient times.

The eastern boundary is clearly defined by the dense tropical rainforests of Anioksait. These rainforests, with their hot and humid climate, crisscrossing rivers, and many unique plant and animal species, form a natural ecological barrier. The southern terrain is predominantly mountainous. These mountains, though not as towering as those in the west, feature complex topography with interwoven valleys. Particularly noteworthy is the southwestern edge of the Qimiak region, which gradually transitions into a vast, endless golden desert. This desert experiences extreme temperature differences between day and night and frequent sandstorms, forming the empire's southernmost natural boundary.

docs.title: Climate: The Novaya with Non-Coincident Rain and Heat
docs.text: The core region of Trusk primarily experiences a climate with non-coincident rain and heat, but the north is also influenced by ocean currents from the North Sea, resulting in a temperate monsoon climate in parts. The southern index region is largely subtropical, with many jungles and broadleaf forests. Between the southernmost part and Long Island lie vast savannas, difficult to traverse. The southeast has extensive primeval forests and humid, hot plateaus.

docs.title: Vegetation
docs.text: The Mediterranean climate zone has a rich variety of plant types. The jungles and deserts of the southwest and southeast have their corresponding vegetation.

docs.title: Fauna
docs.text:
Complex animal biomes resulting from multiple climate types. Meat livestock (poultry) mainly include chickens, ducks, sheep, and pigs; draft animals are primarily the Tapir-Luo (horse) and Ruiba (cattle).

The Novaya Sea has abundant fishery resources. There is a great variety and quantity of fish, along with various seafood such as scallops and mussels.

The Trusk Basin has near-river biome ecosystems along its rivers. The variety of fish within the rivers is not extensive. Meanwhile, most near-river areas have developed agriculture on the basis of the original ecology, and the area of cultivated land has continuously expanded with the advancement of farming techniques.

docs.title: Language
docs.text:
Standard Truskean is based on the Heimet dialect. The main characteristics of the Heimet dialect are: larger mouth openings, stress often placed on the penultimate syllable, and a falling tone at the end of words.

Truskean has numerous dialects, but they can mainly be categorized into the Laprio dialect and the Northern dialect. The Laprio dialect is widely used in southern Trusk and incorporates a large number of ancient Laprio loanwords. The Northern dialect is primarily led by Truskean speakers among the Glömezher nobility, featuring many phonetic characteristics of the Glömezher language and a more rugged auditory impression.

For a more detailed description of dialects, please refer to the document Truskean.

docs.title: Culture
docs.text: Overall, Truskean culture values freedom and a spirit of adventure, but also emphasizes family and honor. This is due to Trusk's economic and political forms.

docs.title: A Nation Built on Commerce: The Merchant Culture of Trusk
docs.text:
Trusk has had agricultural and forging production forms since ancient times. However, after entering the Heimet Dynasty period, due to Heimet's special geographical location and some legacies of the political undercurrents of the first Kuileot Dynasty, many nobles rose from merchant backgrounds. Furthermore, the shipping and trade system led by Heimet has always been one of the main economic sources for various Truskean dynasties, giving Heimet's official merchants a relatively high status within the empire. As for private merchants, their status varied by era. When the situation in Novaya and Laprio was unstable, unregistered merchants were often treated as "pirates," a status that constantly changed according to the specific needs of the ruler.

But in summary, the official support for commerce from Trusk, the favorable climatic conditions of Novaya, the excellent shipbuilding skills of craftsmen, and the improved sealed longboats based on "magic" provided many conveniences for the commercial activities of the Trusk Empire. This also gave birth to a spirit that崇尚自由和探索 (values freedom and exploration). The folk customs were relatively relaxed, with diverse folk activities and many festivals related to navigation, which also had some connection to the Sea God信仰.

Simultaneously, the Heimet Strait possesses many natural deep-water harbors. Ships from the Xinzhi Sea and Novaya gather here, and goods from all over the empire are distributed here. From beyond the empire, sable furs from the northern frontier, fine steeds from the Horse tribes, handicrafts from Iying, and ironware from Kuotai also arrive in succession. The various skills and arts催生 by the flourishing commerce also spurred people's search for culture and knowledge, profoundly influencing the course of Truskean civilization.

docs.title: Divine Right of Kings: Sole Supremacy on the Sea and Under Heaven
docs.text:
Trusk's religion and aristocratic culture are two sides of the same coin. With Sea God信仰 as the core religion, combined with the aristocratic culture meticulously crafted by the Heimet family, the two互为表里 (complement each other), assimilating and controlling the thoughts of people across the empire for over a thousand years. Of course, after entering the imperial era, the mainstream ideology of the empire inevitably became rigid, shifting towards strengthening centralization of power as its core, and degenerated into an important tool for the皇室 to control the entire nation, especially the core Trusk region.

The basis for the divine right of Truskean kings is based on this consensus: the legitimate emperor of Trusk will definitely become a Drowned God after death. That is to say, the legitimacy of the Truskean emperor stems from people's trust in his divinity, but this divinity in turn stems from the emperor's legitimacy, thus achieving a circular论证 for the legitimacy of Truskean imperial power. This theory actually lacks a solid source of legitimacy, so the dynastic changes in Trusk were not a rare occurrence. The origin of this theory is believed to be recorded in the lost scriptures of the Sea God religion, and there is much circumstantial evidence suggesting it may have formed during the first Kuileot Dynasty, but many details are now不可考据 (unverifiable).

Consequently, despite Trusk having a relatively open atmosphere and a spirit that崇尚自由、探索 (values freedom and exploration), the hierarchical system in Trusk, from the city-state era to the imperial era, was still quite strict, let alone during the period when the Glömezher ruled Heimet. The legitimacy of Truskean nobility, in the early period, still stemmed from their control over their territories. After the Sea God religion spread throughout the nation, it gradually tended to be granted by the emperor.

In Truskean culture, the family is also considered a very important part. Truskean society values filial piety and传承 (inheritance/legacy). For a long time, it also believed in the saying "like father, like son," which may be related to the occupational household registration system briefly implemented during the second Heimet Dynasty.

docs.title: Sea God Religion
docs.text:
The indigenous religion of Trusk is a polytheistic religion centered on the worship of Sea Gods. In the earliest city-state period, Trusk was not coastal, and the deities they供奉 (worshipped) were likely mainly mountain gods and forging gods. Entering the era of the city-state alliance, after expanding their territory to the Heimet Strait and Novaya, the Sea God信仰 gradually dominated the religious system of the entire region. In the religious context, the "sea" is not merely the physical sea itself as a body of water, but a philosophical concept, symbolizing both the chaos at the world's beginning and the flow of fate and nature like water.

The core of the Sea God信仰 is six main gods: God Soriwar, God Gyua, God Noikpi, God Pwaso, God Taciap, God Ugwat. The first three deities are also called the Upper Three Gods, the gods who created the sea, corresponding to the origin of the world in Heimet-Trusk mythology: the world was originally一片鸿蒙 (a vast primordial chaos). The first consciousness to awaken was God Soriwar, who wove the boundless sea. God Gyua then awoke, hiding half of the sea woven by Soriwar. God Noikpi "turned over" the hidden sea, thus creating land and mountains in the world. The latter three deities are called the Lower Three Gods: God Pwaso manages shallow seas, mudflats, and beaches; God Taciap manages deep seas and weather; God Ugwat manages rivers and land. In some local records, the Lower Three Gods are considered avatars of God Noikpi, but the more mainstream view is that they are the共同子 (shared children) of the Upper Three Gods, their "shadows." These six gods may be related to the gods worshipped by the earliest city-states that joined the Trusk city-state alliance, but their prototypes are now difficult to考证 clearly. The verifiable inscriptions are only those from the Matashoksa Gate period, carved on many stone tablets along the Matashoksa Gate大道 (avenue), containing information about the initial theogony.

After the third Heimet Dynasty, the Heimet皇室 attempted to standardize the religion, prescribing祭祀方法 (ritual methods) and the jurisdictions of each deity. God Soriwar presides over生育 (birth/fertility), God Gyua over死亡 (death), God Noikpi over命运 (fate), God Pwaso over战事 (warfare), God Taciap over行路 (travel/journeys), and God Ugwat over丰收 (harvest). A special calendar专门记载 (specifically records) the seasons for祭祀各神明 (worshipping each deity).

Beyond the six main gods, there are also gods representing various natural elements, such as the Sun God, Moon God, Wind God, etc. These gods are all under the管辖 (jurisdiction) of their respective main gods. The Johuzai of the Laprio people have also been partially incorporated. The origins of these gods may be the deities worshipped by early city-states.

Outside the core of Sea God信仰 lies the Drowned God信仰. The Truskeans believe that those who died at sea and returned have become gods, and their names are recorded and列入神册 (entered into the divine register). Consequently, the mainstream Truskean funeral is a sea burial. People sail to the center of the sea, scatter the ashes into the ocean, or cast out stone coffins containing the body. This ritual is also called "藏海" (Hiding in the Sea).

docs.title: The Portesdrahu Dynasty
docs.text: The Portesdrahu Dynasty was a dynasty of the Trusk Empire. It ended the Third Dynasty of Heimet through an assassination, ushering in an era where Glömezher nobility ruled the empire. This dynasty greatly promoted ethnic integration within the Trusk Empire.

docs.title: References
docs.text: Portesdrahu is a concept in the world of Laaerad. It can refer to a family surname within the Trusk Empire, or to a dynasty of the Trusk Empire.

docs.title: Etymology
docs.text: Portesdrahu is a word that exists in both Glömezher and Truskean, originating from Proto-Northeastern Laaerad. Portes in Northeastern Laaerad likely means "foresight" or "precognition," while "drahu" is a Glömezher suffix used to derive occupational nouns that take an object. In summary, the meaning of Portesdrahu approximates "prophet" or "seer."

docs.title: Kuileotkuileot Dynasty
docs.text:
The dynasty established by Kuileot lasted for approximately eight hundred years and is referred to by later generations as the Kuileot Dynasty. Interestingly, the names of the first few kings who succeeded to the throne were not directly related to Kuileot; for example, one was named "The Roast Meat Man (Leukkiapleukkiap)". It was only after the fourth king that the name Kuileot began to be prefixed to all monarchs' names, and consequently, the dynasty came to be known as Kuileot.

The social and economic foundations during this period were relatively primitive. Agricultural productivity was limited, insufficient to sustain a long-lasting and stable rule. Incursions by barbarian tribes occurred from time to time, and the exchange of goods had not yet developed into a mature trade system. Although the core political system was maintained, the inscriptions regarding royal succession are vague, making it impossible to determine whether it was a hereditary system based on bloodline or some form of abdication system. This unstable foundation sowed the seeds for the dynasty's decline.

The end of the Kuileot Dynasty stemmed from a combination of internal and external factors. Inscriptions indicate that the dynasty's final period likely experienced a prolonged drought, dealing a heavy blow to the already fragile agricultural base. Simultaneously, doubts arose within the court regarding the legitimacy of the reigning king's bloodline. These internal troubles and external threats ultimately triggered a massive upheaval, leading to the rapid collapse of the Kuileot Dynasty.

docs.title: Etymology
docs.text:
Kuileot is also a concept in Ancient Truskean, referring both to the first historically documented king in Truskean history and to the dynasty he founded, which lasted about eight hundred years. The word itself holds multiple meanings in the Truskean language and history, potentially referring to a deity or wealth, and it also became a symbol of royal authority.

There are several speculations regarding the etymology of the term Kuileot:

  • Name of a Deity: One hypothesis suggests that an early city-state conquered by the Truskeans worshipped a deity named Kuileot. During the prolonged process of unification, this divine name gradually evolved into a general term for the nobility of that city-state. However, this name does not appear in later Truskean-Heimet pantheons, leaving this hypothesis lacking direct evidence.
  • Meaning "Gold": Another mainstream view posits that in Ancient Truskean, the original meaning of Kuileot was "gold". This word might have initially been a general term for merchants who frequently used gold as currency. Some of these merchants later became political strategists, playing a significant role in the wars that unified the Trusk Empire.
  • Alternative Title for "King": Building on the "gold" hypothesis, another interpretation is that Kuileot was a form of address for "king", symbolizing "the person who possesses the most gold in the world".

All these theories point to one fact: Kuileot, during the era of Ancient Truskean city-states, represented an important political force with immense influence, a force that ultimately coalesced into King Kuileot himself.

docs.title: King Kuileot
docs.text:
King Kuileot is the first recorded monarch in Truskean history. Prior to this, Truskean civilization seemingly did not have the habit of recording history, although some scholars speculate that older rulers might have existed, but their records may have been lost due to some catastrophic event.

According to unearthed inscriptions, during his reign, Kuileot collected treasures from across the land, issued decrees, and constructed palaces. One of his most significant achievements was launching the decisive Heimet War. Although the inscriptions do not detail the specifics of the war, they mention that it "nearly exhausted" the treasures he had amassed, hinting at its brutality. After conquering Heimet, Kuileot became the overlord of all city-states from Trusk to the two great rivers of the strait, laying the foundation for the Trusk Empire.

docs.title: LaprioLaporio Dynasty
docs.text:
The Laprio Dynasty (Laprio Dynasty) was a unique regime in Tusk history, established by the southern Laprio people. Founded during the late period of the [[Heimet Dynasty|First Heimet Dynasty]], it represents a rare era in imperial history where a southern civilization conquered the northern political center of Hasit City in a reverse conquest.

Unlike the traditional bloodline-based aristocratic politics of the north, the Laprio Dynasty was distinctly characterized by a co-governance of commercial oligarchs and warlords. Its brief yet glorious reign had a profound impact on the subsequent economic and political landscape.

docs.title: Historical Background
docs.text:
By the end of the Heimet Dynasty's rule, the northern core region was in decline due to prolonged court infighting and agricultural减产 caused by cold climate. Meanwhile, the southern Laprio people, leveraging the agricultural红利 brought by the hot and humid climate (multiple rice harvests per year) and a surplus from maritime trade, rapidly surpassed the north in national strength.

Southern merchant guilds, having amassed enormous wealth, allied with powerful warlords. Seizing the opportunity presented by the weakened northern defenses, they marched north. After a series of swift and decisive victories, the Laprio coalition forces captured the imperial capital, Hasit City, declaring the temporary end (or forcing into exile) of the Heimet family's rule and establishing the Laprio Dynasty centered on southern interests.

docs.title: Political System
docs.text:
The political structure of the Laprio Dynasty was unique in Tusk history, exhibiting characteristics of an early "Republican Monarchy":

  1. Elective Monarchy:

    Although the supreme ruler of the dynasty was titled "King" or "Emperor," the position was not strictly hereditary by bloodline. The monarch was typically elected by a council composed of top warlord magnates and major merchants (such as the La Valau family), and historical research suggests there may have been fixed term limits.

  2. Co-governance by Merchants and Warlords:

    The traditional Tusk military aristocratic ethos was suppressed during this period, replaced by a merchant culture that valued contracts and profit. The upper echelons of power in the court were firmly held by a few families who monopolized grain transportation and mineral extraction.

  3. Laissez-faire Governance:

    The government adhered to a principle of "limited management." Apart from strictly regulating grain reserves, strategic materials, and necessary corvée labor conscription, the central government rarely intervened in the daily administration of local city-states or private commercial activities. This allowed the empire's commercial vitality to reach its peak during this era.

docs.title: Economy and Society
docs.text:
• Agricultural Hegemony: The dynasty established a massive grain corridor connecting the north and south, continuously transporting surplus grain from southern Laprio to the north. This served as a political tool to control the old northern aristocracy.

  • Cultural Impact: The southern Laprio dialect and subtropical primitive polytheism entered the north, leading to intense collision and fusion with the traditional inland culture of Tusk.

docs.title: Decline and Fall
docs.text:
The downfall of the Laprio Dynasty stemmed from its overinflated expansionist ambitions. To eliminate northern security threats and open trade routes further north, the dynasty's last emperor defied the ancestral principle of "valuing commerce over warfare" and personally led a massive army to campaign against the northeastern index.

This war bogged down in the cold Mistnatila region. The last emperor,不适应 to the extreme cold climate, fell ill and died in the army (some say from plague or battle wounds). When the news reached the homeland, the originally loose commercial alliance instantly disintegrated. The suppressed old northern aristocracy and remaining Heimet forces seized the opportunity to counterattack. The dynasty rapidly collapsed, and the empire once again entered a period of分裂 and混战.

docs.title: Jaudzya Dynasty
docs.text: The Jaudzya Dynasty was established after the long-dormant Jaudzya army captured Heimet during the late period of the Moswarike Dynasty, a time marked by the decline of "magic" and chaos within the imperial court. The first monarch, Tika I, attempted to promote centralizing reforms. However, his son, Yechika, emerged victorious in the power struggle and dismantled his father's vision through enfeoffment, leading to the collapse of the reforms. After the death of Jaudzya Yechika, the state fragmented, and Trusk once again descended into civil war.

docs.title: Related Entries
docs.text: Entry Relation Moswarike Dynasty The Jaudzya Dynasty is the successor dynasty to the Moswarike Dynasty Trusk Empire The Jaudzya Dynasty is a dynasty within the Trusk Empire

docs.title: MoswarikeMoswarike Dynasty
docs.text:
The Moswarike Dynasty, also known as the Guluo Dynasty or the Horse Clan Dynasty, was a unified dynasty in Truskean history established by the northern index.

This dynasty was founded by Tuluhaike, the tenth Great Chieftain of the Guluo people. It marked the first time the northern nomadic civilization comprehensively conquered and ruled over the southern agrarian and maritime civilizations. The Moswarike Dynasty was renowned for its powerful "Spirit Language" magic legions, unique slave-soldier system, and harsh intelligence-based rule. Although its methods of governance were relatively crude, it objectively greatly promoted the integration of bloodlines and cultures between the northern and southern peoples of the Trusk Empire.

docs.title: Rise and Unification
docs.text: The dynasty's founder, Tuluhaike, was a powerful ruler in Guluo history. He ended the long-standing tribal conflicts on the Guluo steppe, unified the various tribes, and was elected as the tenth Great Chieftain (Sajiepiaomake). To accumulate strength, Tuluhaike adopted an extremely pragmatic and low-profile diplomatic strategy in the early years of his rule. On the surface, he submitted and paid tribute to the then imperial hegemon, lulling their vigilance, while secretly training troops and reforming the military system in the north.

docs.title: Southern Conquest
docs.text: When the time was ripe, Tuluhaike led the reformed Guluo army southward. In the decisive battle against the southern Basib Dynasty, the Guluo army demonstrated overwhelming superiority. In addition to elite cavalry, Tuluhaike deployed Spirit Speakers (mages) on a large scale. Taking advantage of the period when the "Snapshot" was far from the origin and the magical tides were high, the storms and lightning summoned by the Guluo people shattered the southern allied forces' defenses. Subsequently, the Moswarike army captured Hasit City, entered the Heimet region, and continued pushing south, conquering most territories including those south of index, establishing a vast empire.

docs.title: Decline and End
docs.text:
The later period of Moswarike Dynasty rule faced a dual crisis:

  1. "Magic" Decline: As the world's planes shifted (the Snapshot returned), the ambient spiritual tides began to recede. The effectiveness of the Spirit Language techniques, upon which the dynasty relied to maintain its rule, significantly weakened, and its military advantage was lost.
  2. Internal Corruption and Truskeanization: The Guluo nobles who entered the south rapidly became corrupt and split from the conservatives who remained in the north. Ultimately, under the counterattacks of rebel forces and old noble factions across the land, this dynasty built on military force and magic came to an end.

docs.title: Coexistence of Enfeoffment and Centralization
docs.text: Tuluhaike implemented a reformed enfeoffment system, distributing conquered lands to meritorious generals and submissive southern nobles, but maintained high-pressure control in core regions.

docs.title: Slave-Soldier System
docs.text: To counter the power of traditional clan elders, Tuluhaike abolished the monopoly of tribal militias. He vigorously protected the basic rights of slaves and selected strong individuals from among them to establish a standing army independent of the Council of Elders. This slave legion was loyal only to the emperor himself, possessed extremely high combat effectiveness and discipline, and was the foundation of the dynasty.

docs.title: Intelligence Rule: Molofa
docs.text: The dynasty established an intelligence and surveillance agency named "Molofa" (meaning "arrow"). This organization reported directly to the emperor, monitoring the vast bureaucracy and local lords, thereby strengthening the central government's reign of terror over the expansive territory.

docs.title: Society and Culture
docs.text:
• Crude Governance: As conquerors, the Moswarike Dynasty's administration was relatively rough. Many of the south's sophisticated agricultural and commercial networks were initially damaged but gradually recovered in later periods.
  • Ethnic Fusion: To consolidate rule, the dynasty encouraged and even enforced intermarriage between Guluo and Truskean people. During this period, a large number of Guluo vocabulary was integrated into the Truskean language, and the lifestyles and customs of the north and south underwent an irreversible, deep fusion.
  • Spirit Language Technology: This was the golden age of Spirit Language research. The imperial family funded extensive research on magic, astrology, and herbs. Many classic works on the Spirit Language were compiled during this period.

docs.title: Tyesuku Second Dynasty
docs.text: The Tyesuku Second Dynasty was established after Tyesuku's Dezhuohei III led an uprising and captured the imperial capital. This dynasty implemented comprehensive reforms to the empire's administrative, military, and economic systems. It clarified the system of primogeniture and stipulated that territories would revert to the central government if a lord died without an heir. Administratively, the regions near the capital Heimet were designated as the Heimet Principality, governed by the emperor or a trusted imperial prince serving as the Prince of Heimet, implementing unified administrative management; the old territories of Tyesuku were established as the Kingdom of Tyesuku, governed by a collateral line serving as the King of Tyesuku, implementing a feudal system; the Laprio Region was administered by the King of Laprio, and the Annan Administrative Office was established in the south to manage colonial affairs; the King of Gulo led the various clans, maintaining tribal or early feudal systems. Militarily, military authority in the capital was separated; apart from the military in index retaining the original tribal system, each kingdom established a standing army. The Heimet Region was completely dominated by the standing army, while other regions still relied mainly on feudal levies. Economically, Tusk society, having experienced turmoil, had lost its unified monetary system. The imperial court resumed minting coins in the Heimet Region, implementing a single monetary tax system and simultaneously taxing commercial transactions; in index, taxes were collected in the form of material exchange; in other regions, due to years of continuous turmoil, the monetary system had collapsed for many years, and taxes in kind were predominant. At this time, [[Tusk]], despite varying government forms and differing customs and cultures across regions, finally began to be connected under a unified, centralized government, marking the formation of the "true empire."

docs.title: Heimet First Dynasty
docs.text: Initiated by Matasioksamen II ascending the throne as the son-in-law of the Kuileot family. During this period, the empire achieved significant development in agriculture, commerce, and military affairs. The reforms of Matasioksamen I laid the foundation for the dynasty's rule, improved the tax system, and constructed major roads and ports, successfully repelling invasions by the Andon people and the North Sea pirates.

docs.title: 883 B.C. Matasioksamen II
docs.text: The Matasioksamen Highway was completed, the Port of Borsolasom was constructed, and centralization of power was further strengthened in the Greater Trusk region.

docs.title: 870 B.C. Lieukti
docs.text: Second son of Matasioksamen II, who gained the throne through rebellion. Achieved advantages in the war against Laprio.

docs.title: 868 B.C.
docs.text: Third son of Matasioksamen II, younger brother of Lieukti. During his reign, he promoted administrative reforms, making court affairs more disciplined.

docs.title: 855 B.C. Loshula
docs.text: Eldest son of Shiniuyangkou, continued his father's policies, maintaining a peaceful and stable political situation.

docs.title: 853 B.C. Matasioksamen III
docs.text: Second son of Loshula, historically assessed as a tyrannical ruler. During his reign, he repeatedly waged wars against Guluo, conquered the land of Zhaoxiasi, and implemented ethnic massacres. He initiated the harem system of the Heimet court and began castrating prisoners of war and slaves to serve as eunuchs.

docs.title: 845 B.C. Boraspon
docs.text: Illegitimate son of Matasioksamen III, legitimized under the harem system. He was an outstanding military strategist who conquered the land of Krusofa and built the Fortress of Tupero. He also launched three attacks on the Ruilian land, decisively defeated the Laprio people, established the city of Boraspon, and mined gold.

docs.title: 835 B.C. Boraspon II
docs.text: Eldest son of Boraspon, constructed the coastal highway to Krusofa, but lost the Ruilian land and its gold mines during his rule.

docs.title: 830 B.C. Grusabot
docs.text: Boraspon II had no sons, so his nephew Grusabot succeeded him, with his maternal line being the Visyufon family. This marked the beginning of eighty years of maternal relative dominance in the Heimet Dynasty.

docs.title: 822 B.C. Mapetumat
docs.text: Younger brother of Grusabot, whose reign was deeply mired in power struggles between maternal relatives and princes. He was mediocre, and inscriptions record little about him.

docs.title: 817 B.C. Ebumakt
docs.text: Prince Yepken, cousin of Boraspon II, gained the throne through military achievements. He plotted a rebellion, deposed Mapetumat, and installed Ebumakt, the son of Boraspon's legitimate daughter, as ruler.

docs.title: 816 B.C. Mapetumat (Restored)
docs.text: Ebumakt was assassinated after only ten months on the throne by the maternal relative Visyufon Nottsot. To counterbalance Yepken, Nottsot mobilized the Turha army. Yepken fled to Geqiwa, leading to a stalemate from the Fortress of Peturo to the land of Krusofa.

docs.title: 815 B.C. Tamog
docs.text: Mapetumat was assassinated by the prince faction, and his young son Tamog ascended the throne. At that time, Nottsot and Yepken reached an agreement to check each other. Upon Tamog's personal rule, he fostered the merchant class to balance the two major powers and reformed the bureaucratic system in the index core regions, promoting bureaucrats as confidants and incorporating them into the power balance. During his over forty-year reign, imperial power was unprecedentedly strengthened, becoming one of the periods of most concentrated imperial authority in Heimet history.

docs.title: 771 B.C. Tamog II
docs.text: Sixth son of Tamog, originally named Jilanmie, changed his name after ascending the throne. He continued to promote the construction of the bureaucratic system based on his father's work but lacked his father's skill and boldness. In the mid-to-late period, imperial power gradually declined.

docs.title: 760 B.C. Aguijio
docs.text:
Younger brother of Tamog II. Early in his brother's reign, Aguijio, as the "Crown Prince Brother," gained command of the Trusk core legion—the Turha army. Exploiting Tamog II's indecisive character, he gradually eliminated the influence of the Visyufon family in the court through a series of purges, ending the family's decades-long shadow rule. Ultimately, in a political coup known as the "Winter Court Assembly," he forced his brother to grant him full regency powers.

During his reign, Aguijio displayed a strong personal will. To thoroughly solve the financial source problem in the south, he launched the famous "Ruilian Land Meat Grinder War." He captured the Ruilian land twice, regained control of the gold mines, and attempted to establish permanent military colonies there. However, due to overly long supply lines and fierce guerrilla resistance from the local natives, both conquests ultimately ended in forced withdrawal due to financial collapse.

Aguijio ruled for 50 years. For the first 40 years, he was the absolute dictator of the empire, known as the "Uncrowned Emperor." However, in the last decade, as he aged and weakened, political power gradually flowed to his political allies and the crown prince. In his later years, he existed more as a symbol.

docs.title: 690 B.C. Tamog III
docs.text:
Son of Tamog II, nephew of Aguijio (some say he was Aguijio's adopted son). Records of Tamog III's reign represent a significant "gap" in history. According to archaeological excavations and unofficial histories, during his reign, a brutal "Struggle for the Gate" civil war erupted within the Heimet family, a conflict between the direct descendants of Aguijio and those of Tamog II.

This civil war led to the burning of the Royal Archives in the capital Hasit, resulting in the loss of大量 official historical materials. Tamog III was likely a mediocre monarch who attempted to balance the two factions and ultimately died in a palace coup. After his death, due to the decline and loss of prestige of the direct imperial bloodline, various feudal lords refused to pledge allegiance to a single monarch, forcing the state into a noble republic status. Although the monarchical title was nominally retained, real power had been stripped away.

docs.title: 650 B.C. Republican Period (Rule by Seven Families)
docs.text:
During this period, Trusk entered a 62-year era of oligarchic politics. The highest state authority was the "Senate" composed of seven major powers:

  1. The Heimet family and its branches: The imperial family split into Heimet, Heimet-Baajalai, and Heimarit, checking each other.
  2. The Visyufon family: Making a comeback as an indispensable force in the imperial center, relying on deep marriage networks and long-standing political power.
  3. The Ruilian family: Local power holders controlling southern trade and some mineral resources.
  4. The Zhaoxiasi family: Formerly the conquered, now entering the core circle through mercenaries and military force.
  5. The Basib family: An emerging family of major merchants and usurers, controlling the state's financial lifeline.

During this period, centralization collapsed, and each family operated like an independent kingdom within its territory. While large-scale civil war was avoided, administrative efficiency was extremely low, external expansion completely halted, and Trusk's influence began to shrink from the frontiers.

docs.title: 598 B.C. Zhuoaoxi
docs.text:
In the late Republican period, a political genius emerged from the Heimet family—Zhuoaoxi. He astutely exploited the conflicts of interest among the other four major families, adopting a strategy of "winning over some, suppressing others."

  • Family Unification: Through marriage and assassination, he forcibly merged the two largest factions of the Heimet family, unifying the imperial voice. For the third (weaker) faction unwilling to submit, he relocated the entire clan to the southern border. This "Southern Heimet" branch later survived tenaciously in the wilderness, becoming the spark for the Salonic restoration centuries later.
  • Political Compromise: He reached the Hasit Secret Pact with families like Visyufon and Basib, recognizing their vested interests and hereditary land rights in exchange for their support in abolishing the republic and restoring the monarchy.
  • Establishment of Dictatorship: Zhuoaoxi did not directly proclaim himself emperor but established the lifelong position of "Chief Consul," effectively exercising imperial power. He redrew the boundaries of the Trusk core region, forcibly incorporating wavering city-states on the periphery into the territory through military colonization, laying the foundation for the empire's later standard borders.

In his later years, Zhuoaoxi became paranoid and senile, embarking on large-scale palace construction and purging former allies, leading to renewed political turmoil.

docs.title: 520 B.C. Zhuoaoxi II
docs.text:
Eldest son of Zhuoaoxi. He was an idealistic monarch who, upon ascending the throne, attempted to revoke the privileges his father had promised to the major families in his later years. He implemented the "Second Centralization Reforms," intending to restore the imperial glory of the Tamog era.

However, times had changed. Nourished during the Republican period, families like Visyufon and Basib had grown into unshakeable behemoths. Faced with Zhuoaoxi II's order to reduce feudal power, several major families quickly formed an alliance and, together with the militarily powerful Zhaoxiasi family, launched the "Senate Coup."

Outnumbered, Zhuoaoxi II fled in haste. He was betrayed by former subordinates during his exile and ultimately died on the desolate road to Krusofa.

With the death of Zhuoaoxi II, the over 360-year-old Heimet First Dynasty came to a complete end. The Trusk Empire subsequently disintegrated, and the southern foreign Laprio people moved north, beginning the foreign-ruled Novaya-Laprio Dynasty.

docs.title: 65 B.C. Yingukho
docs.text:
After a long period of dormancy, the Heimet family launched a coup under the leadership of family leader Yingukho. Exploiting the chaos and decline at the end of the Jaudzya Dynasty, Yingukho led the family's private army and defecting imperial guards to storm the palace, ending the Jaudzya family's rule and formally establishing the Heimet Second Dynasty. After ascending the throne, Yingukho quickly quelled disturbances around the capital and issued proclamations to various provinces, announcing the Heimet family's restoration.

After stabilizing the core ruling area, Yingukho adopted a pragmatic attitude towards the nomadic Guluo tribes on the border. After several probing military conflicts, he signed a treaty with the barbarian chieftain, establishing the line of the great swamp and forest west of the strait as the border and arranging a marriage between the imperial family and a barbarian princess. This move not only pacified the border troubles but also provided the newly established dynasty with precious time for recuperation and reconstruction.

docs.title: 48 B.C. Laiswi
docs.text:
After Yingukho's death, his son Laiswi succeeded. Faced with the cultural fragmentation and religious confusion accompanying post-war recovery, Laiswi dedicated himself to spiritual unification. He implemented moderate religious reforms, presided over the compilation of new scriptures, and established a polytheistic system with the Sea God as the supreme ruler, supplemented by six main deities. This successfully integrated originally conflicting local beliefs and Guluo totems into a unified imperial theological framework.

Politically, Laiswi established a highly inclusive mixed political system. He restored the centralized administrative bureaucracy in the imperial core region, enfeoffed meritorious generals as lords in newly conquered areas under a feudal system, and granted authority to tribal chieftains on the borders, striving to use the improved Sea God religion to maintain border stability. This system effectively balanced various forces at the time, promoted the integration of the Guluo people and the Trusk people, and brought long-term political stability to the empire.

docs.title: 26 B.C. Oleman
docs.text:
Oleman's reign was the period of the Second Dynasty's greatest national strength, historically known as the "Silver Age" (sometimes this term refers collectively to the reigns of these three emperors). Benefiting from the peace and institutional foundations laid by the first two monarchs, Oleman vigorously promoted economic construction. He repaired ancient irrigation systems, promoted drought-resistant crops introduced from the Guluo people, significantly increasing grain yields. Simultaneously, he encouraged foreign trade; Trusk merchant fleets controlled the surrounding seas, the Trusk silver coin Guye became the common currency, and the national treasury was unprecedentedly full.

However, prolonged peace and prosperity also bred hidden dangers. In Oleman's later years, the power of local feudal lords and the priestly class had grown excessively due to long-term accumulation, land annexation was severe, and although central finances were abundant, control over localities began to decline. When Oleman died, the seemingly prosperous empire was actually undercurrents涌动, with various forces deeply intertwined, setting the stage for the next ruler's radical reforms.

docs.title: 5 B.C. Ansait
docs.text:
Towards the end of Oleman's reign, a terrifying plague ended the prosperity of the "Silver Age." The empire's population plummeted to two-thirds of its original size, social structures collapsed, and warlords rose everywhere. Emperor Oleman insisted on inspecting the plague-stricken areas and ultimately contracted the disease and died. The throne was vacant; the eldest son had died young, and a succession struggle erupted between the second son Hetuke and the deeply favored third son Ansait. In a country where primogeniture was the norm, the taciturn but decisive Hetuke抢先 controlled the capital Hasit city's guards, confining Ansait deep within the palace. The metropolitan guards and prince's army supporting Ansait were投鼠忌器 and had to temporarily compromise.

Amidst the chaos, the young leader of the Tyesuku family, 德卓黑一世 (Decoheim), emerged. During this period, he inherited his missing father's position and went to the southern fleet to stabilize the situation. As contact with the center was lost, he independently gathered old forces in the south, establishing a foothold in Eastern Laprio. When Hetuke was besieged by rebels in Hasit city, Decoheim led his army north to relieve the siege. He discovered the confined Ansait in the palace, conspired with him, and rescued him to the south.

Ansait declared his accession in the south, appointed Decoheim as chancellor, and to consolidate the political alliance, married his sister to Decoheim (rumored that Ansait deeply loved his sister, but this painful marriage became the legal basis for Decoheim's future seizure of power). Together, they defeated Hetuke in the north, reuniting most of the empire's territory. However, after the victory, the two had major disagreements on governance philosophy: Ansait favored a looser feudal system, while Decoheim advocated centralization and military dictatorship.

In the fifth year of Ansait (also the year before Decoheim's new era),矛盾激化. Decoheim ultimately策划暗杀了 Ansait and, using his status as the Heimet family's son-in-law, forcibly assumed the empire's highest power. Ansait's death marked the substantive end of the Heimet Second Dynasty. The following year, Decoheim abolished the old calendar, established a new dating system, designated as Trusk Year 1, also the first year of the [[Tyesuku Dynasty]], opening a new era belonging to him.

docs.title: Heimet Third Dynasty
docs.text: After the death of Tyesuku Decoheim, Heimet Fatuo I ascended, initiating the Heimet Third Dynasty. However, his rule sparked conflicts with the nobility and the forces of Vatsukasem I. Ultimately, Fatuo I was assassinated at the "Sunset Banquet," and the dynasty随之终结.

docs.title: Table
docs.text: Year (B.C.) Achieved advantages in war against Laprio. 868 Shiniuyangkou Third son of II Internal Reform: Promoted administrative reforms, disciplined court affairs. 855 Loshula Eldest son of Shiniuyangkou Maintenance: Continued father's policies, peaceful and stable politics. 853 Matasioksamen III Second son of Loshula Tyrant/Expansion: Conquered Zhaoxiasi and massacred; established harem and eunuch system. 845 Boraspon Illegitimate son of III Military Genius: Legitimized and succeeded. Conquered Krusofa, built Fortress of Tupero, defeated Laprio three times, built Boraspon city, mined gold. 835 Boraspon II Eldest son of Boraspon From Prosperity to Decline: Built coastal highway, but lost Ruilian land and gold mines. 830 Grusabot Nephew of II Maternal Relative Rule: Maternal line was Visyufon family, began 80-year序幕 of maternal relative interference. 822 Mapetumat Younger brother of Grusabot Mediocrity: Deeply mired in struggles between maternal relatives and princes, few achievements. 817 Ebumakt Grandson of Boraspon Puppet: Installed via coup by Prince Yepken. 816 Mapetumat (Restored) Turmoil: Maternal relative Nottsot assassinated Ebumakt, restored Mapetumat.
